Name: efficient_inference_optimization
Title: Empirically Optimizing Inference Efficiency for Large Language Models on Commodity GPUs
Short Hypothesis: By combining model quantization, optimized memory management, and dynamic workload allocation, we can significantly improve the inference efficiency of large language models on commodity GPUs, achieving a better latency-accuracy Pareto frontier.
Related Work: Recent works such as Memory-Efficient Speculative Decoding and CPU-GPU Workload Distribution have demonstrated the potential for optimizing inference efficiency on commodity GPUs. Our proposal builds upon these ideas by exploring the combination of model quantization, optimized memory management, and dynamic workload allocation to further improve inference efficiency.
Abstract: Large language models have achieved remarkable performance in text generation, but their computational requirements pose significant challenges for deployment on commodity hardware. This proposal aims to optimize the inference efficiency of large language models on commodity GPUs through a combination of model quantization, optimized memory management, and dynamic workload allocation. We will investigate the use of quantization techniques, such as product quantization, and optimized memory management strategies to reduce memory usage and improve throughput. Additionally, we will explore dynamic workload allocation techniques to efficiently distribute computation across the CPU and GPU. Our goal is to achieve a better latency-accuracy Pareto frontier, enabling the deployment of large language models on commodity hardware while maintaining their performance.
Experiments: Evaluate the effectiveness of model quantization techniques, such as product quantization, on reducing memory usage and improving throughput.
Investigate the impact of optimized memory management strategies, such as memory-efficient speculative decoding, on inference efficiency.
Explore dynamic workload allocation techniques to efficiently distribute computation across the CPU and GPU.
Conduct experiments on a range of large language models, including LLaMA-7B, to demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach.
Measure the latency-accuracy Pareto frontier achieved by our approach and compare it to existing methods.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The complexity of large language models may limit the effectiveness of our approach.
The need for significant computational resources to conduct experiments and evaluate our approach.
The potential for our approach to introduce additional overhead, such as increased memory usage or computational complexity, which could negate its benefits.
